title: Cat Walking with Legs Crossed (Scissor Gait): Causes and Urgency
meta_desc: Is your cat walking with crossed legs? Learn about scissor gait, why it is a high-priority emergency, and how to assist your veterinarian.
content_html:
What is Scissor Gait in Cats?

A scissor gait occurs when a cat's hind limbs cross over each other while walking, mimicking the motion of scissors opening and closing. This is not a behavioral quirk but a significant clinical sign of ataxia, specifically indicating that the cat is unable to properly sense the position of its limbs. This is most often linked to a disruption in the spinal cord or the neurological pathways that control motor function.

Is it an Emergency?

Urgency Level: High. A scissor gait is a serious medical symptom that requires immediate veterinary evaluation. It often points to severe underlying issues such as spinal cord compression, intervertebral disc disease (IVDD), tumors, or even a Saddle Thrombus (a blood clot blocking blood flow to the hind legs). If your cat is also vocalizing in pain, has cold paws, or is dragging its limbs, please head to the nearest emergency animal hospital immediately.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

When you contact a vet or arrive at the clinic, providing a short video of your cat attempting to walk can be invaluable. Cats are masters at hiding illness and may become stationary or "freeze" due to the stress of being at a veterinary office. A video taken in a comfortable home environment captures the exact nature of the gait, allowing the medical team to quickly localize the neurological lesion and prioritize your cat for immediate care.

faqs.question: Can a scissor gait resolve on its own?
faqs.answer: No, a scissor gait typically indicates a physical or neurological obstruction that will not improve without medical intervention and could worsen into total paralysis.

faqs.question: What will the vet do to diagnose the issue?
faqs.answer: The vet will perform a neurological exam to check reflexes and pain perception, and may recommend diagnostic imaging like X-rays, an MRI, or a CT scan.

faqs.question: Is scissor gait the same as limping?
faqs.answer: No. Limping usually involves favoring one leg due to pain, whereas a scissor gait involves a lack of coordination and crossed-limb placement affecting both hind legs.
